title: How to know when you are ready for deeper work
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The question I hear more than any other

Someone writes to me, or sits across from me, and their voice gets quiet. They say, "I think I'm ready for the deeper work. But I'm not sure."

Here's the thing. That uncertainty itself? It's not a sign you aren't ready. It might be the first real sign that you are.

I've been there. I spent years waiting for some magical green light. Some internal feeling of total readiness that would tell me, "Okay, now you can handle it. Now you're strong enough to look at what's underneath."

That light never came. Because it doesn't exist.

What I found instead was something quieter. Less dramatic. A shift in how I related to my own survival. Let me walk you through what that actually looked like. And what it might look like for you.

The false readiness trap

The wellness industry loves selling you the idea that you need to be "ready" before you can heal. That there's a right time, a right state, a right version of yourself that will somehow make the work easier.

Bullshit.

What actually happens is that people use the concept of readiness as a shield. A way to stay safe without admitting they're scared. I did this for years. I'd read another book, take another workshop, journal another page, and tell myself I was "preparing." But I wasn't preparing. I was hiding.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about this in terms of pendulation. The nervous system naturally moves between activation and settling. Between approaching the wound and backing away. That backing away isn't failure. It's part of the rhythm.

But there's a difference between pendulation and permanent retreat. Between taking a breather and building a fortress.

Recovery isn't linear. I know you've heard that before. But I want to say it differently. Recovery isn't just "not linear" - it's actively confusing. You'll have a week where everything feels possible and then a day where you can't get out of bed. You'll think you've healed something and then it shows up again wearing a different costume. That's not failure. That's how it works.

Pete Walker talks about "emotional flashbacks" - those periods where you're suddenly flooded with the feelings of childhood without any visual memory attached. You just feel small, helpless, worthless, terrified - and you don't know why. These can last hours or days. And they can happen years into recovery. That doesn't mean you haven't made progress. It means there are layers. There are always more layers.

What I've learned is this. The goal isn't to never be triggered again. The goal is to recover faster. To recognize what's happening sooner. To have more tools available. To be gentler with yourself when you fall. That's progress. Not perfection. Not the absence of pain. Just a different relationship with it. A relationship where you're not alone in it anymore.

Here's how I learned to tell the difference. When I was genuinely pendulating, I could feel the pull toward the deeper work even as I stepped back. There was a tension, a knowing, a sense that I'd return. When I was just hiding, the stepping back felt like relief. Like a door clicking shut. And I'd fill that space with more preparation, more research, more "getting ready."

Think about it. Have you ever caught yourself doing that? Reading another book about trauma instead of sitting with your own body's sensations? Watching another YouTube video about attachment theory instead of noticing how you feel in a relationship right now?

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link). It's a book that can help you understand the difference between intellectual understanding and embodied knowing. I recommend it often. But I also warn people: don't use it as another way to avoid the actual work.

The body tells you first

Your mind will lie to you about readiness. Your body won't.

I learned this from Deb Dana, who studies the polyvagal theory in clinical practice. She talks about how the nervous system communicates through what she calls "glimmers" and "triggers." Glimmers are micro-moments of safety. Triggers are micro-moments of threat. Your body is sending these signals constantly.

When you're actually ready for deeper work, your body starts sending different signals. Not necessarily comfortable ones. But different ones.

Here's what I noticed in myself. I'd be sitting in my living room, and suddenly I'd feel a wave of sadness come through. Not overwhelming. Not flooding. Just present. And instead of reaching for my phone or turning on the TV, I'd feel a curiosity about it. Like, "Oh, there you are. I see you."

That's a readiness signal.

Another one: I'd be in conversation with someone, and they'd say something that would normally send me into a freeze response. But instead of going numb, I'd feel my feet on the floor. I'd notice my breath get shallow, but I'd also notice I could take a deeper one. The freeze was still there. But it wasn't total.

That's another readiness signal.

And here's the weird one. Sometimes readiness feels like a kind of boredom with your own coping strategies. You've been using the same defenses for years. They worked. They kept you alive. But suddenly they feel... tired. Old. Like clothes that don't fit anymore.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker writes about exactly this phenomenon. The moment when your survival strategies stop feeling like protection and start feeling like prison. That's not a crisis. That's an opening.

The myth of the perfect window

I used to think I needed a perfect window of time. A weekend with no obligations. A therapy retreat. A period in my life where everything else was calm so I could focus solely on healing.

That window never came. Life doesn't work that way.

What I learned instead is that deeper work happens in the margins. In the fifteen minutes before bed when you're too tired to keep your defenses up. In the moment after a difficult conversation when your nervous system is already activated and you can choose to stay with the sensation instead of numbing it. In the car ride home from work when a memory surfaces and you don't immediately change the radio station.

Janina Fisher, who specializes in treating structural dissociation, says that healing happens in small doses. Not in dramatic catharsis. Not in some big breakthrough moment. But in the tiny, almost invisible choices you make to stay present with yourself.

So readiness isn't about having a perfect window. It's about recognizing that the windows are already there. You just haven't been looking through them.

Here's a practical way to check in with yourself. Ask this question: "If I had one hour of complete privacy and safety right now, what would I want to do with it?"

If your answer is something like "sleep" or "watch TV" or "eat comfort food," you might not be ready. Those are legitimate needs. Honor them.

But if your answer is something like "sit with that feeling I keep pushing away" or "write about that memory that keeps showing up" or "call my therapist and talk about what really happened," then you're probably more ready than you think.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It gives you concrete exercises for exactly this kind of check-in. I've used it myself when I wasn't sure what I was feeling or whether I was ready to feel it.

What readiness actually feels like

Let me be honest with you. Readiness doesn't feel like confidence. It doesn't feel like strength. It feels like a quiet resignation. Like finally admitting to yourself that the cost of not doing the work is now higher than the cost of doing it.

That's what it was for me. I was so tired of being afraid of my own history. So exhausted from the constant vigilance required to keep the past in the past. I finally just... gave up. Not in a defeated way. In a surrendered way. Like, "Fine. I'll look at it. I'll feel it. I don't care anymore if it breaks me. Because I'm already broken by not looking at it."

That's readiness.

It's not a feeling of capability. It's a feeling of inevitability. Like the work is going to happen whether you choose it or not, so you might as well choose it.

You might also notice that your symptoms start to shift. Not get worse, necessarily. But change. Maybe the anxiety that used to live in your chest moves to your throat. Maybe the numbness that used to cover everything starts to crack in specific places. Maybe you find yourself crying at things that never made you cry before, or feeling anger that you never allowed yourself to feel.

These are signs that your system is reorganizing. That it's making space for something new. It can feel scary. But it's actually a good sign.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). Reading it might bring up feelings you've been avoiding. That's not a reason to stop reading. That's a reason to keep going, slowly, with support.

How to start without feeling ready

So you've read all of this. And maybe you're thinking, "Okay, I recognize some of these signs. But I still don't feel ready. What do I do?"

Here's my answer. Start anyway. But start small.

Don't try to process your biggest trauma on day one. Don't sign up for a week-long intensive if you're not sure you can handle a single session. Start with the body. With the breath. With the simple practice of noticing what you're feeling without trying to change it.

Set a timer for three minutes. Sit somewhere comfortable. Close your eyes. And just notice. Notice the temperature of the air on your skin. Notice the weight of your body in the chair. Notice any sensations that arise without judging them or trying to make them go away.

If you can do that for three minutes without dissociating or panicking, you have enough readiness to go deeper.

If you can't, that's okay. That's information. It means you need more stabilization work first. More grounding. More resources. That's not failure. That's part of the process.

Stay with me here. The goal isn't to be fearless. The goal is to be brave enough to feel the fear and still take one small step forward.

And here's the thing I wish someone had told me years ago. You don't have to do the deeper work alone. In fact, you shouldn't. Find a therapist who understands complex trauma. Find a support group. Find a friend who can for you without trying to fix you. The deeper work happens in relationship, not in isolation.

You are ready enough. You have always been ready enough. The only question is whether you're willing to feel the fear and do it anyway.

I think you are. I think that's why you're still reading.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - Validates the experience of growing up with parents who couldn't meet your emotional needs.
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - Structured exercises for navigating the healing process with C-PTSD.
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma through the lens of neuroscience and lived experience.
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's guide for understanding and recovering from complex trauma.

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
